narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model's prediction toward expecting you to struggle on the bar exam, since it falls notably below the 3.24 average among students who passed on their first try. Next in influence was your first-year grade decile of 3, which also signaled lower early academic performance compared to the typical 6th decile for successful bar takers, further tilting the odds against you. While your LSAT score of 40 was a bright spot—actually stronger than the 37 average for those who passed—it wasn't quite enough to offset those concerns and pulled in the opposite direction toward success. Overall, the model estimated a 67% chance you'd fail the bar on your first attempt, which is why your law school application was rejected; the admissions process prioritizes applicants predicted to pass and contribute positively right away. Your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who succeed, and opting for full-time study matches the norm for passers, but those didn't carry enough weight here. Compared to accepted students, your profile shows solid potential in some areas like the LSAT, yet the early academic hurdles made you riskier in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your determination or background—it's just how these key metrics stacked up. Many students in similar positions have turned things around by targeting weaknesses head-on. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your GPA and decile rankings, proving you can excel academically. You might also seek targeted LSAT-level prep to maintain that edge while building a stronger overall record. Reapplying after demonstrating improvement could shift the prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing; your story isn't over yet.
